trie_description: Explore the visualization tool for Trie prefix tree operations. Interactive animation demonstrates the insertion, search, and deletion operations of words, providing a intuitive understanding of the working principle of Trie trees.
linkedlist_description: Linked list is a linear data structure that consists of a sequence of elements, each element points to the next element in the sequence. This page shows the process of insert, delete and search for elements in the linked list.
stack_description: Stack is a linear data structure that follows the Last In First Out (LIFO). This page shows the process of push, pop and adjust the height. Dynamically displays the detailed content of stack operations, helping to understand stack operations.

hashtable_description: Hash table is a data structure that implements an associative array abstract data type, a structure that can map keys to values. This page shows the process of insert, delete and search for key-value pairs in the hash table.

skiplist_description: Skip list is a data structure that allows fast search within an ordered sequence of elements. This page shows the process of insert, delete and search for elements in the skip list.

dpcoin_description: Visualization of the dynamic programming coin change algorithm. Set the target amount and the available coin denominations, click the step-by-step calculation button, and observe how the optimal solution is found step by step. hashring_description: The hash ring visualization of consistent hashing allows you to clearly and intuitively understand the key value allocation of consistent hashing, and add key value migration for deleting nodes. It also supports setting up virtual nodes (shadow nodes) to understand the effect of distribution balance optimization.

bloomfilter_description: Bloom filter is a space-efficient probabilistic data structure that is used to test whether an element is a member of a set. This page allows you to visualize the process of adding keys to the bloom filter and checking for key existence.

jumphash_description: Jumphash is a consistent hashing algorithm that provides a balance between the number of keys assigned to each node and the number of nodes. This page allows you to visualize the process of adding keys to the jump hash and checking for key existence.
kmp_description: Interactive KMP visualization that teaches both prefix table construction and efficient pattern matching. Step through LPS building, mismatch fallback, and final match positions.
kmp_ready: Ready to explore how KMP builds the prefix table and reuses it during matching.
kmp_inputs_updated: Inputs changed. Please generate the steps again.
kmp_pattern_required: Please enter a pattern before running KMP.
kmp_prefix_ready: Prefix steps are ready. Auto Play starts immediately, and you can pause to inspect them step by step.

kmp_reset_done: Visualization reset. You can load an example or type your own strings.
kmp_prefix_table_description: Watch how the LPS array is built from repeated prefix and suffix structure inside the pattern.
kmp_pattern_row_description: The current pattern that all helper arrays are derived from.
kmp_lps_row_description: Modern implementation view: longest proper prefix length that is also a suffix.
kmp_next_row_description: A common textbook fallback table. This page uses the convention next[0] = -1.
kmp_nextval_row_description: Skips some fallback positions that would immediately compare equal pattern characters again.
kmp_matching_description: Follow how the text pointer and pattern pointer move without restarting from the beginning after every mismatch.
kmp_theory_intro: These arrays describe the same KMP fallback idea from slightly different historical and implementation viewpoints.
kmp_convention_note: Many textbooks define next in slightly different ways. To keep the comparison precise, this page uses a common classic convention where next[0] = -1.
kmp_main_playback_note: The step-by-step animation on this page is driven by LPS because it is clearer in modern code, while next and nextval are shown as theory and comparison aids.
kmp_controls_hint_idle: Click a generate button to prepare steps, then watch them with Step or Auto Play.
kmp_controls_hint_lps_ready: Prefix steps are ready. Use Step to continue one move at a time, or Auto Play to keep them moving.
kmp_controls_hint_lps_playing: Prefix steps are playing. Pause any time if you want to inspect them step by step.
kmp_controls_hint_match_ready: Matching steps are ready. Use Step to continue one move at a time, or Auto Play to keep them moving.
kmp_controls_hint_match_playing: Matching steps are playing. Pause any time if you want to inspect them step by step.
kmp_lps_initialize: Start with LPS[0] = 0. The first character has no proper prefix that is also a suffix.
kmp_lps_match_step: Characters matched at i = {{index}}. Extend the known prefix length to {{length}} and write it into the LPS array.
kmp_lps_fallback_step: Mismatch while building LPS at i = {{index}}. Reuse previous work by falling back from len = {{from}} to len = {{to}}.
kmp_lps_zero_step: No prefix can be reused at i = {{index}}, so this LPS value stays 0.
kmp_lps_complete_step: The prefix table is complete. KMP can now use it to skip unnecessary comparisons.
kmp_match_initialize: Start matching with both pointers at the beginning of the text and pattern.
kmp_match_advance_step: Characters '{{textChar}}' and '{{patternChar}}' matched at text[{{textIndex}}] and pattern[{{patternIndex}}]. Move both pointers forward.
kmp_match_fallback_step: Mismatch at text index {{textIndex}}. Instead of restarting, move the pattern pointer from {{from}} back to {{to}} using LPS. In classic textbook notation, this is the same fallback idea described by next or nextval.
kmp_match_shift_step: Mismatch with pattern pointer at 0, so advance the text pointer to the next position.
kmp_match_found_step: A full match ends here. Record a match starting at index {{index}} and continue with the LPS fallback.
kmp_match_no_result_step: Matching complete. No occurrences of the pattern were found.

dynamicChartsIndexDoc:
Here is a collection of important data charts that change over time. Each chart shows the key data trends in different fields, allowing you to intuitively understand the historical changes and future trends of various indicators.

The charts cover multiple fields, including but not limited to:
- Population statistics: Show the population growth trends of countries around the world
- Economic indicators: Show the changes in GDP, inflation rate, etc.
- Technology development: Show the evolution of technology indicators such as internet users, smartphone penetration rate, etc.
- Social development: Show the trends of social progress indicators such as education level, medical care coverage, etc.

Each chart uses a dynamic bar chart race to make the data change process more intuitive and vivid. Some public data also provides download links for further analysis and research.

You can also use your own private data to generate customized dynamic charts.

population-growth.title: World Population Change (1800-2015)
population-growth.intro: The world population has shown a continuous growth trend since 1800, with population growth accelerating significantly towards the end of the 19th century and the beginning of the 20th century. The Industrial Revolution in the late 19th century and the economic development it brought improved living conditions in most countries globally, leading to accelerated population growth. From the mid-20th century, developing countries such as China and India experienced rapid population growth. In contrast, high-income countries saw a gradual slowdown in population growth rates after entering the 20th century.

income-growth.title: Global Income Change (1800-2015)
income-growth.intro: In 1800, the income in the United Kingdom was already several times higher than many countries, with the Industrial Revolution greatly widening the gap between high-income countries and others. Compared to industrialized nations, countries like India, China, and some African nations saw little change in income levels for most of the 19th century. The rapid development of emerging economies such as China and India in the late 20th and early 21st centuries has gradually brought these countries' income levels closer to those of high-income countries. Although global income has grown overall in the latter part of the 20th century, income disparities between high-income and low-income countries still persist. The change in global income has experienced a hugely unbalanced development, initially dominated by the income growth of developed countries driven by the Industrial Revolution, and later transformed by the rise of emerging economies, but significant global income disparities remain.

china_gdp.title: China GDP Change (2004-2023)
china_gdp.intro: China's economy has experienced significant growth over the past 20 years. Taking Guangdong Province as an example, its GDP increased from 1,865.83 billion yuan in 2004 to 13,567.32 billion yuan in 2023, growing approximately 6.3 times. By comparing growth rates across different regions and periods, we can observe an overall trend of slowing economic growth. For instance, Guangdong Province's average annual growth rate was about 14.4% during 2004-2013, while it decreased to about 8.0% during 2014-2023. This indicates that China's economy is transitioning from a high-speed growth phase to a high-quality development phase. Beijing and Shanghai, as municipalities directly under the central government, have relatively high total GDP, which may reflect their status as centers for finance, technology, and service industries. Even in the face of global economic challenges (such as in 2020), most regions maintained positive growth, demonstrating the resilience of China's economy.

china_population.title: China Population Change (2004-2023)
china_population.intro: The analysis reveals a clear divergence in population trends across China's provinces. Eastern coastal regions such as Beijing, Guangdong, and Shanghai show significant population growth, with average increases ranging from 1.5% to 2%. These areas attract large numbers of migrants due to their economic prosperity and abundant job opportunities. In contrast, northeastern provinces like Heilongjiang and Jilin are experiencing negative population growth, with Heilongjiang seeing the most significant decrease (-1.15%). These regions are facing industrial decline and population outflow. Some central and western provinces, such as Guizhou and Gansu, are also seeing slower population growth. Future demographic changes will likely focus on the relationship between urbanization and regional economic disparities. Eastern economic centers may continue to attract populations, while provinces with less diversified economies may struggle with population loss.

china_income.title: China Income Change (2004-2023)
china_income.intro: The analysis reveals significant regional disparities in fiscal revenues across Chinese provinces, with coastal provinces like Guangdong and Jiangsu far outperforming inland provinces such as Tibet and Qinghai, reflecting uneven economic development. Nevertheless, most provinces showed a continuous growth trend from 2004 to 2023, demonstrating China's overall economic progress. However, recent years have seen a general slowdown in growth rates, possibly reflecting the impact of economic transformation and structural adjustments. Notably, some provinces like Guizhou and Tibet have experienced rapid fiscal revenue growth, likely tied to national regional development strategies and support policies. Meanwhile, provinces with diversified economic structures, such as Guangdong and Zhejiang, have shown stronger resilience to economic fluctuations, highlighting the importance of industrial diversity for fiscal stability.

china_outcome.title: China Expenditure Change (2004-2023)
china_outcome.intro: The analysis reveals a continuous growth trend in fiscal expenditure across Chinese provinces, reflecting the government's increasing investment in people's livelihoods and economic development. However, there are significant regional differences in the scale and growth rate of expenditures, with economically developed eastern coastal provinces like Guangdong and Jiangsu far outspending western provinces like Qinghai and Ningxia, highlighting the vast disparity in fiscal strength. Notably, some central and western provinces such as Sichuan and Guizhou have seen faster expenditure growth, possibly linked to national strategies for balanced regional development.

china_population_more.title: China Birth Rate Changes (2012-2023)
china_population_more.intro: Changes in the birth rate of various provinces and cities in China in different years. From 2012 to 2023, the national birth rate showed a gradual downward trend. The birth rate in 2012 was 12.10‰, and by 2023 it dropped to 6.39‰. There are obvious regional differences in birth rates. The birth rate in Tibet has always remained at a high level. In contrast, the birth rates in big cities such as Shanghai and Beijing are lower, with 3.95‰ and 5.63‰ respectively in 2023. Although the two-child policy was relaxed in the middle, it can be seen from the data that the birth rate did not increase significantly, but continued to decline in the following years. Generally, provinces with more developed economies, such as Guangdong and Zhejiang, have relatively low birth rates.

imagemetadata_explain_image_description: Image description field. It is commonly used for a photo title, caption, purpose, or short content summary.
imagemetadata_explain_artist: Artist or creator field. It may store the photographer, designer, copyright owner, or image creator.
imagemetadata_explain_copyright: Copyright field. It can store the rights owner, license note, usage restriction, or copyright statement.
imagemetadata_explain_make: Camera or device manufacturer field, such as Canon, Nikon, Sony, Apple, or another maker.
imagemetadata_explain_model: Camera or device model field, such as an iPhone model, camera body, scanner, or capture device.
imagemetadata_explain_lens_model: Lens model field. It usually records the lens name or lens specification used for capture.
imagemetadata_explain_software: Software field. It records the application that created, edited, or exported the image.
imagemetadata_explain_datetime: Date and time field. It usually describes file, capture, creation, or modification time.
imagemetadata_explain_datetime_original: Original capture time field. It usually records when the photo was taken.
imagemetadata_explain_create_date: Create date field. It usually records when the image was digitized, generated, or created.
imagemetadata_explain_modify_date: Modify date field. It usually records when the image content or metadata was last changed.
imagemetadata_explain_orientation: Orientation field. It tells software how the image should be rotated or flipped for correct display.
imagemetadata_explain_exposure_time: Exposure time, also known as shutter speed. It records how long the sensor received light.
imagemetadata_explain_f_number: F-number or aperture value. A smaller value usually means more light and shallower depth of field.
imagemetadata_explain_iso: ISO sensitivity field. Higher values make low-light images brighter but may add more noise.
imagemetadata_explain_focal_length: Focal length field. It records the lens focal length and affects angle of view and perspective.
imagemetadata_explain_flash: Flash field. It records whether the flash fired and may include flash mode details.
imagemetadata_explain_white_balance: White balance field. It records the color correction mode, such as auto or manual white balance.
imagemetadata_explain_metering_mode: Metering mode field. It describes how the camera measured light for exposure.
imagemetadata_explain_exposure_program: Exposure program field. It records the capture mode, such as manual, aperture priority, shutter priority, or program auto.
imagemetadata_explain_exposure_compensat: Exposure compensation field. It records deliberate exposure adjustment made during capture.
imagemetadata_explain_shutter_speed: Shutter speed value field. It is another encoded form of exposure time.
imagemetadata_explain_aperture: Aperture value field. It is another encoded form of lens aperture.
imagemetadata_explain_color_space: Color space field. It explains how image colors should be interpreted, such as sRGB or Adobe RGB.
imagemetadata_explain_profile_descriptio: ICC profile description. It usually names or describes the embedded color profile.
imagemetadata_explain_width: Image width field. It records the pixel width or metadata width value.
imagemetadata_explain_height: Image height field. It records the pixel height or metadata height value.
imagemetadata_explain_compression: Compression field. It describes the compression algorithm or encoding method used by the image data.
imagemetadata_explain_bits_per_sample: Bits per sample field. It describes color precision per channel and can affect dynamic range.
imagemetadata_explain_maker_note: Manufacturer private field. Camera makers store custom data here, and the structure is often proprietary.
imagemetadata_explain_user_comment: User comment field. It may contain notes written by the camera, user, or editing software.
imagemetadata_explain_gps_latitude: GPS latitude field. It records the north-south position of the capture location. Check privacy before sharing.
imagemetadata_explain_gps_longitude: GPS longitude field. It records the east-west position of the capture location. Check privacy before sharing.
imagemetadata_explain_gps_altitude: GPS altitude field. It records elevation information for the capture location.
imagemetadata_explain_gps_date: GPS date field. It usually stores the capture date from the positioning system.
imagemetadata_explain_gps_generic: GPS-related field. It may store location, direction, altitude, timestamp, or positioning data. Check privacy before sharing.
imagemetadata_explain_xmp_generic: XMP metadata field. It is often used by Adobe apps, asset managers, copyright systems, tags, edit history, and extended descriptions.
imagemetadata_explain_iptc_generic: IPTC metadata field. It is common in news photos, stock photography, copyright data, captions, keywords, and publishing workflows.
imagemetadata_explain_icc_generic: ICC color profile field. It helps software keep image colors consistent across devices and applications.
imagemetadata_explain_jfif_generic: JFIF field. It is common in JPEG files and may store density, thumbnail, and baseline JPEG compatibility data.
imagemetadata_explain_ihdr_generic: PNG IHDR field. It stores core PNG header information such as width, height, bit depth, and color type.
imagemetadata_explain_exif_generic: EXIF-related field. It usually stores camera details, capture parameters, time, orientation, GPS, and thumbnail data.
imagemetadata_explain_tiff_generic: TIFF-related field. It usually stores image dimensions, compression, orientation, color, and basic image structure data.
imagemetadata_explain_generic: This is an image metadata field. Different formats, cameras, phones, and editing apps may use different names for source, capture, structure, color, or editing information.
